title: Expressing support for the designation of the week beginning February 3, 2025, as "National Tribal Colleges and Universities Week".
plain_summary: This bill wants to make the week starting February 3, 2025, a special week called National Tribal Colleges and Universities Week. There are 34 colleges that serve Native American students from more than 230 different tribes. These schools provide education that includes Native American culture and traditions, often in areas far from other colleges. The House of Representatives wants all Americans to celebrate these important schools during this week with special activities and programs.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution shows support for making the week that starts on February third, two thousand twenty-five, into National Tribal Colleges and Universities Week.

sections.title: Background Information
sections.text: There are thirty-four tribal colleges and universities that operate on more than ninety campuses across sixteen states. These schools are colleges that receive special permissions to operate from Native American tribes or from the federal government. This gives them a special relationship with the federal government that other colleges do not have. These schools serve students from more than two hundred thirty Native American tribes that are officially recognized by the federal government. Tribal colleges and universities teach students knowledge and skills that are based on Native American cultural traditions and values. This includes teaching Native languages. This education helps strengthen Native American communities and makes the United States richer as a nation. These schools provide high-quality college education opportunities for American Indians, Alaska Natives, and other people who live in some of the most remote and economically struggling areas in the United States. Tribal colleges and universities are officially approved schools that prepare students to succeed in jobs around the world and in today's competitive job market. These schools have open enrollment policies, which means they accept students without strict requirements. About thirteen percent of students at these schools are not Native American. The shared mission and important achievements of tribal colleges and universities deserve to be recognized by the entire nation.

sections.title: House Resolution
sections.text: The House of Representatives supports making National Tribal Colleges and Universities Week official. The House also encourages all people in the United States and interested groups to celebrate National Tribal Colleges and Universities Week with appropriate activities and programs that show support for tribal colleges and universities.
